title: How I Audit a DV360 Account in Under Thirty Minutes
excerpt: When a client hands over a stalled account, they want to know two things in the first meeting: what is wrong, and how quickly it can be fixed. Here is the exact audit I run before that meeting.
body:
When a client hands over a stalled account, they want two things in the first meeting: **what is wrong**, and **how quickly it can be fixed**. Not a strategy deck. Not a quarterly plan. A diagnosis.

Over the last few years I have compressed that diagnostic into a repeatable thirty-minute pass. It fits neatly into the gap between one client call and the next, and it tells me — with maybe eighty percent confidence — where the account is actually leaking.

Here is the audit, in the order I run it.

## 1. Delivery pacing (5 minutes)

Open every active insertion order and check the *Delivery* column against the *Days remaining* column. Anything under ninety percent pacing at the mid-flight mark is a red flag, and anything over one hundred and ten percent is worth investigating too — over-pacing often means bids are set too aggressively and the account will run out of budget early.

> Over-delivery is not a win. It is a budgeting failure with a friendlier name.

At this stage I am not trying to fix anything. I am just building a list of IOs that need attention.

## 2. Frequency caps and audience reach (7 minutes)

The two most common causes of quiet under-delivery are:

- **Frequency caps too tight** — a cap of two per twenty-four hours can filter out huge chunks of the addressable pool by mid-morning.
- **Audience segments too narrow** — a first-party segment intersected with a third-party segment intersected with a device filter often leaves a delivery pool of five hundred users a day.

For each flagged IO, I check the *Reach & frequency* report in DV360. If unique users delivered is far below the estimated reach, the caps or the audience logic is the culprit — not the bid.

## 3. Creative rotation (5 minutes)

In *Creatives → Performance*, sort by impressions descending. If one creative is taking more than sixty percent of impressions in a multi-creative line item, DV360's optimiser has quietly picked a winner and is starving the others. Sometimes that is fine. Often, the "winner" is fatigued and its click-through rate is trending down week over week.

Force even rotation for a control period, and let the numbers tell the truth.

## 4. Floodlight and conversion attribution (8 minutes)

This is where most audits find real money.

- Are Floodlight tags firing on the right pages?
- Are custom variables passing through correctly, or arriving as `undefined`?
- Is the conversion window still set to the default thirty days, when the client's own CRM says the median lead-to-close is closer to nine?

I keep a small snippet handy for quick sanity checks in the browser console:

```javascript
// Check what Floodlight sees on this page
console.log(window.__floodlight_variables__ || 'no floodlight object found');
```

Bad tags do not announce themselves. They just quietly attribute the wrong conversions to the wrong campaigns until someone gets fired.

## 5. Bid strategy sanity check (5 minutes)

Only *now* — with pacing, audience, creative, and tracking checked — do I look at bids. Specifically:

1. Is the bidding strategy aligned to the KPI the client actually cares about? A CPM target when the goal is CPA leads to predictable disappointment.
2. Is the *Bid multiplier* stack doing anything useful, or is it a decade of well-meaning tweaks that cancel each other out?
3. Is the *Optimise for viewability* toggle on, when it does not need to be — and paying a twenty percent premium for it?

## Wrapping up

At the end of thirty minutes I have a one-page diagnostic that reads something like:

- Two IOs pacing under sixty percent — frequency caps and audience narrowing.
- One creative dominating impressions across three IOs — pause and rotate.
- Floodlight custom variable `order_value` returning `undefined` on the checkout page — engineering fix.
- Bid multiplier stack has redundant device and geo layers — simplify.

None of this is glamorous. It is not the deck the client was expecting. But it is what the account actually needs. And the good clients — the ones worth working with — always prefer a diagnosis to a strategy.

title: Why Under-Pacing Is a Diagnostic Problem, Not a Bid Problem
excerpt: A $50,000 near-mistake, avoided by refusing to touch the bid until the actual constraint was found — a lesson in why under-pacing is a filtering problem dressed up as a money problem.
body:
There's a specific kind of panic that sets in on an agency floor when a major programmatic campaign is 48 hours into launch and the budget dial hasn't moved. Working across large enterprise accounts at Dentsu and Merkle Sokrati, I've watched the same knee-jerk reaction play out almost every time: someone panics and doubles the bid. Raising the bid blindly, without knowing why the system isn't spending, is a fast way to waste a client's money. Under-pacing isn't an invitation to pay more — it's a warning that the setup is choked somewhere upstream.

Here's the diagnostic order I run before I ever touch a bid.

## 1. Check the troubleshooter for impression loss (5 minutes)

First stop is the DV360 Intelligence panel or Troubleshooter, checking "Lost to Filtering" against "Lost to Auction." If impressions are being lost because the system isn't even bidding, raising the price changes nothing.

## 2. Test the audience & data size (5 minutes)

Next, I review the targeting parameters. Layer a tight geography on top of a niche first-party list and the addressable pool may simply be too small to scale, regardless of price.

## 3. Check frequency caps & targeting conflicts (5 minutes)

I check frequency limits against brand-safety overlays. An aggressive cap — say, one impression per lifetime — stacked with strict pre-bid filters from DoubleVerify or IAS will choke scale on its own.

## 4. Check inventory & deal health (5 minutes)

For anything bought through a PMP or programmatic guaranteed deal, I check win rate and render rate. Is the publisher actually passing bid requests, or is there a technical mismatch — creative dimensions, macros — silently killing delivery?

## 5. Real example: the $50,000 save

We launched a targeted display campaign for a major B2B brand. Two days in, line items were pacing under 5% of the daily target. The execution desk's first instinct was to spike the CPM from $6 to $15 to force delivery. Instead, I pulled the diagnostics: the system wasn't losing bids on price, it was discarding inventory because the creative was restricted to desktop sizes while 75% of the audience was on mobile — worsened by a one-per-day frequency cap. Introducing responsive HTML5 creative and loosening the cap to three impressions a day brought the campaign to full pace at the original $6 CPM. Raising the bid instead would have cost roughly $50,000 for zero added value.

## Wrapping up

Under-pacing is almost never a money problem. It's a filtering problem wearing a money problem's clothes. Run the diagnostic, find the actual constraint, and only reach for the bid once everything upstream of it is clean.

title: Reading a CM360 Report Like a Media Planner, Not an Ops Person
excerpt: Nine years across agency floors and enterprise client tables taught me the same lesson twice: a specialist who can explain a 0.02% dip in CTR isn't the same as a planner who can tell a client where growth is actually coming from.
body:
Over nine-plus years on both sides of the media table — from execution floors in Pune to high-stakes reviews with global enterprise clients — I've sat through more meetings than I can count where a genuinely talented specialist gets lost in the weeds. The breaking point that made me want to write this down was a review for a global enterprise client where a junior team member spent fifteen minutes diagnosing a tiny fluctuation in click-through rate across static and video placements. I watched the client's face the whole time. Total detachment. They didn't care about a 0.02% shift in clicks — they cared about growth.

Here's the read-order I use instead, top to bottom, every time a CM360 report lands on my desk.

## 1. Ignore the ghost metrics first (2 minutes)

Before anything else, I mentally grey out the columns that measure delivery rather than impact — raw impressions, raw clicks, whether the line item spent out smoothly. Useful for an ops check. Meaningless for a business read. High impressions mean nothing if the placements sat at the bottom of a page nobody scrolled to.

## 2. Check unique reach & frequency (5 minutes)

I don't lead with total impressions in a client conversation — I lead with unique reach. Are we expanding the footprint to new potential buyers, or hammering the same audience ten times over and burning budget on repetition instead of expansion?

## 3. Check net viewable impressions (5 minutes)

Working with large global brands, brand safety and viewability aren't optional. I look past total impressions to net viewable delivery, verified through DoubleVerify or IAS. A served impression nobody could physically see never had a chance to do anything.

## 4. Trace the Floodlight conversion path (10 minutes)

This is where the real read happens. I skip simple last-click attribution and look at the interplay between view-through conversions — people who saw the ad, didn't click, but converted later — and click-throughs, to see how the whole ecosystem is actually working together.

## 5. Real example: the network that looked too good

Early in my career, one programmatic network on a B2B campaign looked like a clear winner on paper — cheap clicks, a click-through rate soaring above our premium direct placements. The ops instinct is to shift budget there immediately. Mapping the conversion paths against real pipeline data told a different story: those clicks were cheap because they were low quality, driven heavily by app placements with a near-zero post-click conversion rate. A seemingly "expensive" contextually targeted placement was quietly responsible for the real, high-value leads.

## Wrapping up

By the end of this read, I have a one-page view that tells me:

- Whether reach is actually expanding or just repeating.
- Whether the impressions we paid for had a chance to be seen.
- Which conversions are real versus attributed by accident of the last click.
- Where the budget should move next.

A CM360 report shouldn't just summarize where your money went. Read correctly, it tells you exactly where your next dollar needs to go.

title: The Quiet Discipline of Floodlight Hygiene
excerpt: A dashboard reporting a spike in conversions while the client's actual sales stayed flat taught me the industry loves talking about algorithmic bidding and pays almost no attention to the plumbing underneath it.
body:
I once inherited a legacy enterprise account where the dashboard reported an incredible spike in conversions — but the client's internal sales database stayed completely flat. Auditing the back-end tagging architecture turned up the reason: multiple legacy Floodlight tags, left behind by past agencies, all firing simultaneously on a single confirmation page. Our industry loves talking about algorithmic bidding. Almost nobody audits the plumbing that feeds it.

Here's the three-step check I now run on a schedule, not just at launch.

## 1. Run the CRM sanity sync (10 minutes)

I pull a raw Floodlight volume report and set it side-by-side against the client's actual internal sales numbers or Google Analytics. A major statistical gap between the two is an immediate flag for a tracking discrepancy or a double-firing tag.

## 2. Calibrate the lookback window (5 minutes)

I check the active attribution settings. Accounts are frequently left running on the default 30-day click/view lookback window. For a fast-moving or low-consideration product, an overly generous view-through window inflates performance that isn't real. I tighten the window to match the client's actual buying cycle.

## 3. Audit the custom variables (10 minutes)

I check whether proprietary data points — customer tier, purchase value, product line — are passing cleanly through the custom (u-) variables. If those strings come back blank or errored, the optimization engine is flying blind and doesn't know it.

## 4. Real example: paying to reach people who already bought

We managed a lead-generation campaign where automated bidding was aggressively scaling spend against a high volume of form completions. Cost-per-lead looked excellent to the untrained eye. A routine hygiene audit found that our custom variables weren't distinguishing existing users logging into their portals from new prospects. The system had learned it could buy cheap ad space by retargeting people who were already customers — and it was doing exactly that, efficiently. Cleaning up the Floodlight logic to isolate true net-new actions saved the client nearly 40% of media spend that had been going toward preaching to the converted.

## Wrapping up

None of these three checks are glamorous, and none take more than ten minutes. But skipping them means every downstream decision — bidding, budget, optimization — is being made on data that might already be lying to you. Clean data beats a clever strategy every single day.
